coredata.dc:title: Yellow-emitting carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for the rapid determination of curcumin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shen
Bao, Jinjia
Lv, Ling
Bai, Yongjie
Zhang, Yiming
Zhang, Yaofang
coredata.dc:description: The non-standard use of curcumin could cause some adverse drug reactions, such as diarrhea, nausea and skin allergies. Thus, the curcumin determination was fundamental to disease treatment and prevention. Herein, a facile and efficient fluorescent probe was developed based on carbon dots, which was prepared through hydrothermal method (o-phenylenediamine and N-isopropylacrylamide as the reaction raw materials). Characteristics of the as-fabricated carbon dots (NCDs) were studied through some analysis techniques, such as UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared instrument, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence quenching phenomenon could be observed after addition of curcumin. This as-prepared fluorescent probe displayed a significant response for the determination of curcumin with a satisfactorily lower detection limit of 0.017 μM and a considerable linear range of 0.5–50 μM compared to reported literatures. Because of the preeminent repeatability and anti-jamming capability, the as-developed CDs suggested mighty potentiality for actual applications of curcumin detection in real samples and temperature sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Curcumin
Fluorescence sensor
Temperature sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of curcumin in real samples
Determination of curcumin and selectivity of this strategy
Sensing of curcumin in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), XPS (B), C1s (C), N1s (D), O1s (E) and FT-IR spectrum (F) of NCDs.
UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of NCDs (A); Effects of excitation wavelength on emission wavelength (B).
Emission spectra of solution with different curcumin concentrations (A); Linear curve (inset: the picture of NCDs solution with the addition of curcumin under daylight (a) and UV light (b)) (B).
UV–vis spectrum of curcumin, excitation and emission spectra of NCDs (A); UV–vis spectra of NCDs with different curcumin concentrations (B); Quenching efficiency (C); Fluorescence lifetime (D).
Emission spectra of NCDs at different temperature (A); Linear curve (B); Cycle curve (C).
Schematic for synthesis of NCDs by hydrothermal method.
Comparison of different analytical methods for curcumin determination.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of curcumin on NCDs.

head.ce:title: Yellow-emitting carbon dots as fluorescent sensors for the rapid determination of curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The non-standard use of curcumin could cause some adverse drug reactions, such as diarrhea, nausea and skin allergies. Thus, the curcumin determination was fundamental to disease treatment and prevention. Herein, a facile and efficient fluorescent probe was developed based on carbon dots, which was prepared through hydrothermal method (o-phenylenediamine and N-isopropylacrylamide as the reaction raw materials). Characteristics of the as-fabricated carbon dots (NCDs) were studied through some analysis techniques, such as UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared instrument, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence quenching phenomenon could be observed after addition of curcumin. This as-prepared fluorescent probe displayed a significant response for the determination of curcumin with a satisfactorily lower detection limit of 0.017 μM and a considerable linear range of 0.5–50 μM compared to reported literatures. Because of the preeminent repeatability and anti-jamming capability, the as-developed CDs suggested mighty potentiality for actual applications of curcumin detection in real samples and temperature sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Fig. S1) was firstly isolated as a low relative molecular weight polyphenol compound from Curcuma longa L. in 1870. The diferuloylmethane structure of curcumin was elucidated in 1910 and followed by significant progress in research on physiological and pharmacological effects [1–2]. Curcumin is a commonly used medicine, and its main bioactive components are curcumins and volatile oils. The former has effects of lowering blood lipids, anticoagulation, antioxidant, cholagogic and anticancer [3–4]. The latter mainly has anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and cough relieving effects [5]. Curcumin exerts its anticancer effects in clinical practice by inducing malignant tumor cell differentiationand tumor cell apoptosis, and inhibiting various stages of tumor growth [6–7]. The non-standard use of curcumin could cause some adverse drug reactions, such as diarrhea, nausea and skin allergies [8]. Thus, the curcumin determination was fundamental to disease treatment and prevention.

In recent years, some detection methods for curcumin concentration have been developed, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9–10], UV–vis spectrophotometry (UV–vis) [11], capillary electrophoresis [12] and electrochemistry [13–15] and fluorescence methods [16–18]. Compared to these strategies, fluorescent methods provide many advantages of excellent sensitivity, easy operation, high stability and fast analysis speed to expand the application scope [19–20]. Thus, a strategy for facile, precise and rapid detection of curcumin is becoming more and more significant in pharmaceutical fields.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a type of carbon nanomaterial, have attracted more and more interest due to their efficient synthesis, rich functional groups, optical characteristics, biocompatibility and water-solubility [21–22]. The as-prepared CDs have been extensively applied in many fields, such as fluorescent probes, catalysis, cell imaging, targeted drug delivery and drug determination [23–25]. Specially, the element hybridization of CDs can improve fluorescence quantum yields and optical feature [26–27]. Therefore, fluorescence measurement by utilizing doped CDs is a significant system for the detection of ions and drugs.

In the study, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) with excellent stability were successfully performed from o-phenylenediamine and N-isopropylacrylamide by hydrothermal strategy. The structures and characteristics of NCDs were discussed through a series of techniques, such as transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR), ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The NCDs displayed excellent sensitivity and selectivity for curcumin determination with a linear range of 0.5–50 μM and detection limit of 0.017 μM. Finally, the NCDs were also applied for curcumin measurement in bovine serum and glucose samples.

o-phenylenediamine (99.5 %), N-isopropylacrylamide (99 %), sodium chloride (99.5 %), potassium chloride (99.5 %), sodium carbonate (99.5 %), glucose (98 %), vitamin C (99 %), uric acid (99 %), tryptophan (99 %), histidine (99 %), glycine (≥99 %), isoleucine (99 %), methionine (99 %), phenylalanine (99 %), alanine (99 %), threonine (99 %), urea (99 %), lactose (98 %), sucrose (≥99.5 %) and curcumin (97 %) were commercially supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs and curcumin were discussed by using a UV-4500 Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence data of NCDs with different concentrations of curcumin were obtained from an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with excitation and emission slits of 10 nm/10 nm. The types and elements states of NCDs were studied through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The morphology of NCDs was discussed by using transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai F30, USA) at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet, is20, USA) was applied to discuss surface functional groups of NCDs. Fluorescence lifetime of NCDs was obtained from FLS1000 (Edinburgh, UK).

NCDs were prepared through hydrothermal method based on related reports [28–29] and the concise schematic was shown in Scheme 1 . In brief, a solution was obtained through mixing o-phenylenediamine (0.2 g), N-isopropylacrylamide (0.1 g) and ultrapure water (30 mL). The above solution was stirred for 30 min until o-phenylenediamine and N-isopropylacrylamide dissolve and introduced into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave under 180 °C for 6 h. The obtained solution was cooled down naturally to 25 °C and centrifuged under 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove insoluble suspended solids. Finally, the developed NCDs solution was placed in refrigerator for further use.

The fluorescence quantum yield of NCDs solution was obtained based on related reports [30–31]. Quinine sulfate was applied as a reference. The absorbance value and fluorescence data were recorded through ultraviolet spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated through the following formula.
QY = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2
where QY and QYR refer to fluorescence quantum yields of NCDs and quinine sulfate; I and IR stand for fluorescence spectrum’s integral areas of NCDs and quinine sulfate; A and AR represent the absorbance of NCDs and quinine sulfate; η and ηR refer to the refractive index of NCDs and quinine sulfate.

For curcumin determination, a fluorescent platform was developed through mixing NCDs solution (20 μL) and phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 6, 1.8 mL) (Fig. S2), and different concentrations of curcumin were dropped into the detection platform. Afterwards, the prepared solution was slowly shaken and incubated for 5 min at 25 °C. When the excitation was set as 410 nm, the fluorescence emission data were obtained through fluorescence spectrophotometer at excitation/emission slit of 10 nm/10 nm. The linear equation and detection limit were obtained through data processing and calculation. To study the selective experiments, some substances (Na+, K+, CO3 2−, glucose, vitamin C, uric acid, tryptophan, histidine, glycine, isoleucine, methionine, phenylalanine, alanine, threonine, urea, lactose and sucrose) were added into NCDs solution under the same conditions. The correspondent fluorescence data were measured under the same excitation wavelength and slits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of curcumin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Bovine serum samples were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose samples were purchased from the pharmacy. The river water was obtained from campus. All samples were diluted 100 times by using PBS buffer solution. Then, curcumin solutions were spiked into the above samples (the final concentrations of curcumin were 10 and 20 μM). The determined concentration and recovery rates were obtained through the processing of fluorescence data.

The as-performed NCDs were excellent dispersed in aqueous solution and displayed yellow fluorescence under UV light. In TEM image (Fig. 1 A), NCDs were spherical and the average particle size was 3.5 ± 0.2 nm through statistics of many particles. The element type and oxidation state of NCDs measured by XPS were displayed in Fig. 1B. Fig. 1B displayed three distinct characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.4 eV and 531.6 eV, which were ascribed to the binding energy of C1s, N1s and O1s. The results suggested that NCDs mainly had C, N and O elements [32–33]. The contents of C, O, N elements from XPS were 47.67 %, 47.29 % and 5.54 %, respectively. The C1s single spectrum was divided into three feature peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.7 eV and 287.9 eV, implying that some groups were observed including C–C, C-N and C = O/C = C bonds (Fig. 1C) [34–35]. The C-N bond could be observed at 399.4 eV in N1s single XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) [36]. The OH, C = O and C-O bonds could be separately discovered at 530.7 eV, 531.8 eV and 535.1 eV in O1s single XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) [37–38].

The FT-IR spectrum of NCDs displayed in Fig. 1F suggested the possible functional groups on NCDs surface. The wide characteristic peak located at 3429.5 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H or N–H bond [39]. The stretching vibration characteristic peak of C = O bond was observed at 1620.5 cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 1353.8 cm−1 and 1043.3 cm−1 were attributed to the deformation vibration of C–H and O–H bonds, respectively [40–41]. The feature peaks located at 1399.6 cm−1 and 1106.2 cm−1 could be attribute to the stretching vibrations of C-N and C-O bonds [42–43]. These functional groups corresponded well with the conclusion of XPS.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence data of NCDs were studied through ultraviolet spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The corresponding spectra were suggested in Fig. 2 A. In UV–vis absorption spectrum, two feature peaks at 288 nm and 431 nm were displayed, which was attributed to π-π* transition of C = C group and n-π* transition of C = O group, respectively. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 410 nm and 555 nm, respectively.

The NCDs solution displayed light yellow and yellow under daylight and UV lamp, which was corresponding with fluorescence emission wavelength (inset in Fig. 2A). Afterwards, the excitation-dependent or excitation-independent feature was confirmed though measuring emission wavelength under different excitation wavelengths. In Fig. 2B, the fluorescence emission intensity changed continuously with excitation varying from 380 to 430 nm, along with emission wavelength basically unchanged. The results suggested that excitation wavelength could not affect emission wavelength. These changes might be ascribed to the uniform particle size and surface structure of NCDs [44–46]. In subsequent experiments, we selected the excitation wavelength of 410 nm for the detection of curcumin. Based on the quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY value was measured to be 17.3 %.

From Fig. S2A, we studied influences of PBS solution’s pH value on fluorescence intensity of measurement system (20 μL NCDs solution and PBS solution). F0 and F correspond to fluorescence intensity of measurement system without and with curcumin. Before adding curcumin, the fluorescence intensity of measurement system displayed little variation when PBS solution’s pH value increased from 6 to 8. After the addition of curcumin, the fluorescence intensity of measurement system presented similar rules. The F0-F value reached maximum value when the pH was 6. Then, we selected the PBS solution of pH = 6 as a subsequent testing condition.

The optimal cultivation time for curcumin measurement was discussed under PBS solutions of pH = 6. The fluorescence intensities of measurement system with curcumin were obtained under the different cultivation time (30 s, 60 s, 90 s, 120 s, 150 s and 180 s). In Fig. S2B, the difference of F0-F value was not significant when the cultivation time increased from 30 to 180 s. Then, 30 s was set as the optimal cultivation time for curcumin determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of curcumin and selectivity of this strategy
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, determination of various amounts of curcumin was studied and relevant results were displayed in Fig. 3 . As we could see from Fig. 3A, fluorescence intensity of measurement system decreased continuously with increasing curcumin concentrations from 0 to 50 μM at the emission wavelength of 555 nm. Meanwhile, a significant linear response situation was obtained through linear fitting of data, and the linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.037[curcumin] + 0.047 with the curcumin concentration range of 0.5–50 μM (R2 = 0.9930) (Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.017 μM based on the calculation of 3σ/k. Inset of Fig. 3B, when the curcumin was added into the NCDs solution, the yellow fluorescence disappeared. Compared with existing reports, this as-developed platform based on NCDs suggested a more significant linear range and lower LOD (Table 1 ). Therefore, the as-prepared NCDs could be utilized as an efficacious fluorescence nanosensor for measurement of curcumin. To carry out the selectivity of curcumin based on NCDs, some reference substances were discussed including Na+, K+, CO3 2−, glucose, vitamin C, uric acid, tryptophan, histidine, glycine, isoleucine, methionine, phenylalanine, alanine, threonine, urea, lactose and sucrose. In Fig. S3, only curcumin possessed a considerable “turn-off” influence on fluorescence intensity, suggesting that the as-developed NCDs held excellent selectivity for curcumin determination.

To suggest the fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism, the static or dynamic quenching was studied through UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence lifetimes. As we can see from Fig. 4 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin and fluorescence excitation spectrum of NCDs possessed an overlap phenomenon, suggesting that fluorescence excitation of NCDs could be absorbed by UV–vis absorption of curcumin. This overlap phenomenon implied the existence of inner filter effect between NCDs and curcumin [47]. In Fig. 4B, when the curcumin amounts increased from 0 to 50 μM, the absorbance of NCDs gradually enhance and the position of absorption peak did not shift, displaying that the fluorescence quenching of NCDs by curcumin was ascribed to interaction between curcumin and NCDs [48]. Moreover, the Parker equation (1) was applied to confirm inner filter effect mechanism [49–50] and the meaning of each parameter was displayed in Table S1.
(1)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em

Based on the Parker equation, the correction factors (CF) of inner filter effect were discussed. The prerequisite for reliable calibration was that the CF values were below 3. The results were performed in Table 2 . The observed fluorescence quenching efficiency (Eobsd) was larger than that the corrected fluorescence quenching efficiency (Ecor) (Fig. 4C). Therefore, the inner filter effect existed.

Furthermore, the above results also could be applied to support the existence of static quenching mechanism. Fluorescence lifetime was applied to confirm the existence of static quenching mechanism. If static quenching mechanism exists, fluorescence lifetime with addition of curcumin does not change within the error range. As displayed in Fig. 4D, the fluorescence lifetime without and with addition of curcumin were 1.90 ns and 1.84 ns, respectively. So the static quenching and inner filter effect were main quenching mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of curcumin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To augment the scope of application, the as-prepared NCDs was applied as a fluorescent nanoprobe for determination of curcumin in real samples. Various amounts of curcumin were introduced into bovine serum, glucose and river water samples, and the concentrations of curcumin in these samples were measured by above linear fitting equation. In Table 3 , recoveries of 91.4–107.5 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.79–3.74 % were obtained, suggesting that the as-developed NCDs could be applied as an underlying replacer for measurement of trace levels of curcumin in real samples.

As we can see from Fig. 5 A, fluorescence intensity of NCDs increased continuously with increasing temperature from 25 °C to 95 °C at the emission wavelength of 555 nm. Meanwhile, a significant linear response situation was obtained through linear fitting of data, and the linear equation was F = 6.86[temperature] + 968.4 with temperature range of 25–65 °C (R2 = 0.9373). Furthermore, only a slight decrease occurred for fluorescence intensity of NCDs under multiple cycles between 25 °C and 95 °C. Therefore, the as-prepared NCDs could be utilized as an efficacious fluorescence nanosensor for temperature sensing.

NCDs with yellow fluorescence were developed through hydrothermal strategy by utilizing o-phenylenediamine and N-isopropylacrylamide as reaction raw materials. The as-synthesized revealed prominent water solubility, excellent dispersibility, significant stability and optical feature with QY of 17.3 %. The as-synthesized NCDs could be utilized to detect curcumin with linear range of 0.5–50 μM and LOD of 0.017 μM. The static quenching and inner filter effect have been proven to be main quenching mechanisms. This fluorescence nanoprobe was applied to determine curcumin in bovine serum, glucose and river water samples with a recovery of 91.4–107.5 % and RSD of 2.79–3.74 %. Compared with reported literatures, NCDs applied as a nanoprobe for curcumin determination is a simple and efficient strategy with significant sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, the as-prepared NCDs could be utilized as an efficacious fluorescence nanosensor for temperature sensing. Therefore, this platform is anticipated to possess more applications.

Shen Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jinjia Bao: Supervision, Software. Ling Lv: Supervision, Software. Yongjie Bai: Software, Formal analysis. Yiming Zhang: Software. Yaofang Zhang: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the College Students’ Innovation Program of Taiyuan Normal University (No. CXCY2221) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123819.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
The structure of curcumin, effects of buffer solution volume, pH and time on the fluorescence intensity, selective experiments and parameter of Parker equation were shown in Supplementary Material.
